risks.risk: Power-infrastructure moats are time-bound; BE's solid-oxide fuel-cell IP may face faster competitive compression from modular nuclear, grid-scale batteries, or utility in-sourcing than SA LP's 2030-to-2040 payoff window allows.
risks.grounding_citation: Mechanize founding thesis (EA Forum, March 2025): value capture during gradual automation concentrates at application + vertical-productization layers over time, not at commodity-input layers. Power generation is a binding constraint (Aschenbrenner IIIa: 100GW by 2030) but binding constraints do not imply durable pricing power once the constraint is visible and the market expands. BE's moat is technological (SOFC architecture), not regulatory or infrastructural; it is subject to technology-risk obsolescence faster than grid operators or utility incumbents face pricing compression.

risks.risk: Ensemble split (Light Street hold vs. Altimeter exit) may signal divergent views on whether the power-constraint narrative prices in the maturation of alternative architectures. Altimeter's exit could read as skepticism on BE-specific execution, not on power-generation demand itself.
risks.grounding_citation: Mechanize's critique of the barbell assumes value capture goes to application/vertical layers. If Altimeter is exiting BE specifically (not power-infra broadly) due to execution concerns, customer concentration, or grid-provider consolidation risk, the divergence is not a regime call (Thorstad's lane) but an asset-selection one. SA LP retained dominant position (6.42% of portfolio after 35.64% trim), suggesting SA LP is NOT reading Altimeter's exit as a binding-constraint-level risk.

adjustment_rationale: BE is a power-infrastructure play, which Mechanize classifies as a bottleneck-provider layer with genuine value-capture durability (unlike foundation-model layer chip leaders). Power is a real binding constraint per Aschenbrenner IIIa (100GW by 2030), and SA LP's thesis that power-infra will compound value during the trillion-dollar-cluster ramp is directionally sound. The fragile_low_gamma regime classification is a Thorstad-framed question (epsilon > beta + low gamma) that governs the position-sizing timing via Loop 3 (cash_raise + NVDA put hedge), not the fundamental value-capture direction. Mechanize's value-capture critique is weak here: infrastructure plays do NOT face the multiples-compression risk that foundation-model-layer chip leaders face as value accrues to application-layer beneficiaries. The ensemble disagreement (Light Street hold, Altimeter exit) does not read as thesis-breaking; it reads as timing/execution preference. Mechanize endorses holds on power-infra; the position belongs in the book at current regime sizing. Risk is low-to-medium (technology-moat compression on 2030+ horizons), not structural. Capital allocation to power-infra is sound; deployment timing is governed by regime/gamma questions (Loop 3 purview), not value-capture-layer reasoning.
